Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD's global scope only defines metadata (pkgname, pkgver, etc.) and the source array, which consists of a desktop file and icon PNGs from `raw.githubusercontent.com` plus a release ZIP from `github.com`. All source URLs point to the project's own upstream repository, which is standard for AUR binary packages. SHA256 checksums are provided for each source file. There is no `pkgver()` function, no top-level command execution, and no obfuscated or dangerous code. The `package()` function contains only installation commands and is not executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. Therefore, running these two commands poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>PKGBUILD sources are benign; no dangerous code executes.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Checksums are provided for every source file, ensuring integrity. The `package()` function only installs the binary, icons, desktop file, and a configuration file to disable the official Telegram Desktop updater — all expected operations for this package. There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous commands, or any behavior that deviates from normal packaging. No evidence of a supply-chain attack.
</details>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources: a local desktop file, multiple PNG icons fetched from the project's GitHub repository under a known path, and a versioned release ZIP archive from the official GitHub releases page. All URLs are over HTTPS and point to the declared upstream (https://github.com/TDesktop-x64/tdesktop). The `source` array contains no command substitution, function calls, or untrusted network requests. There is no `pkgver()` function, so no dynamic version calculation occurs. The top-level scope contains only safe variable assignments. No code is executed during sourcing beyond what is necessary for makepkg to determine sources and their checksums. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only fetch the listed sources and parse metadata, with no risk of remote code execution or data exfiltration at this stage.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in sourcing or fetching sources</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD script is a standard AUR packaging script for distributing a pre-compiled binary of the 64gram-desktop application. It fetches the upstream source from the declared GitHub repository URL using a versioned release (v1.2.5), which is a trusted and expected practice. The source consists of a desktop file, several icon files, and a zip archive containing the binary—each with corresponding SHA-256 checksums provided, ensuring integrity.

No suspicious or malicious behaviors are present. There are no uses of `curl|bash`, `eval`, or any kind of obfuscated code. The script does not execute arbitrary downloaded content, nor does it exfiltrate data. The operations in the `package()` function are limited to extracting and installing the binary, icons, and desktop file into standard system directories, which is normal for an Arch Linux package. Additionally, it disables the upstream updater by placing a configuration file in a designated directory—a common practice to maintain control over updates via the package manager.

All dependencies and optional dependencies are legitimate and relevant to the application's function as a desktop messaging app. The package conflicts with itself to prevent multiple installations, and it provides a virtual package name for dependency resolution. These are all standard and safe packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for io.github.tdesktop_x64.TDesktop.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard .desktop entry for a desktop application, defining metadata, launch commands, and actions for the 64Gram Desktop application. It specifies the application name, comment, executable path (64gram-desktop), icon, and integration with the desktop environment (e.g., categories, MIME type handling for tg: links). The "Quit Telegram" desktop action uses the `-quit` flag to exit the application cleanly.

There are no scripts, network operations, file manipulations, or executable commands outside the scope of standard desktop entry functionality. The Exec lines reference the application itself with safe command-line arguments, and no obfuscated or suspicious code is present. All fields follow freedesktop.org .desktop file specifications and align with expected behavior for integrating a messaging application into a Linux desktop environment.

This file does not execute arbitrary code, download content, or access sensitive system resources. It solely serves to register the application in the user's desktop environment.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/64gram-desktop-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-qn6eb46k...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD has no top-level command substitutions, no `pkgver()` function, and no code that executes during sourcing beyond simple variable assignments and array definitions.

The `source` array fetches from three locations, all tied to the package's declared upstream (`https://github.com/TDesktop-x64/tdesktop`): a local `.desktop` file already in the AUR repo, icon PNGs pulled from the project's own `dev` branch on GitHub, and the release ZIP from the project's own GitHub Releases page. All nine entries have explicit `sha256sums` (no `SKIP`), which provides integrity verification at download time.

One minor hygiene note: the icon PNGs are fetched from the mutable `dev` branch ref rather than a pinned commit. However, because checksums are present and enforced by makepkg, any unexpected change would cause a checksum mismatch and abort the build. This is a best-practice concern, not a security threat.
</details>
<summary>Standard binary package; all sources from upstream GitHub with checksums.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard binary package; all sources from upstream GitHub with checksums.[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices throughout. All sources are fetched from the legitimate upstream repository (`https://github.com/TDesktop-x64/tdesktop`): icon files are pulled from the project's `dev` branch (a minor hygiene note since it's a mutable ref, but sha256sums are provided for integrity verification), and the release binary zip is pulled from the project's official GitHub Releases page. All nine sources have explicit sha256 checksums — none are set to SKIP.

The `package()` function performs only routine operations: creating installation directories, installing the binary, desktop file, and icons into `$pkgdir`, and writing a small configuration file to disable the app's built-in updater (standard practice for distribution-packaged apps so the package manager handles updates). There are no network calls at build/install time beyond the declared sources, no use of `eval`, `base64`, `curl|bash`, or any other suspicious constructs, and no file operations outside of `$pkgdir`.

No evidence of injected malicious code, data exfiltration, or execution of attacker-controlled content was found.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR binary package; all sources from legitimate upstream with checksums.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

There are no shell commands, scripts, network requests, encoded payloads, or anything beyond normal desktop integration metadata. All `Exec` entries reference the package's own binary (`64gram-desktop`), which is expected behavior.
</details>
